the FB Manager onsite loop is 5 interviews - similar to the IC route. IIRC, the loops are called : a/tech retro|strategy, b/product intuition, c/strategic influence, d/people management, and d/leading@fb. Remember, people management at FB is very much a "supportive role" - you serve as a guide most of the times and are less likely to be extremely prescriptive about what someone on your team will be doing. 

1/ tech strategy : similar to the IC round, and done by an eng leader. walk thru the tech details of a program you led/influenced. key to remember is talking about what frameworks you use to guide your team members. Also, how do you influence eng leaders at your peer levels on system/product decisions, etc. Focus on data, impact, etc. are key elements - so make sure your examples cover those aspects. 

2/ PI : similar to IC system design. usually done by an M2. same overall guidance: use of high level frameworks, systemic thinking. not necessarily looking to go into the depth of system architecture. decision making, trade-offs, overall product sense, goals, business impact, understanding of diff tech options, knowing when to ask for help, etc.

3/ strategic influence : usually done by a PM leader. leadership influence w/o authority, driving collaboration, building relationships, handling conflicts, hard conversations, understanding formal and informal org structures, etc. org savvy & driving change, etc. 

4/People Mgmt : done by m1/m2 TPM. focus on your leadership style, org design, building and growing your team, hiring strategies, perf management (high and low), career growth (great if you have grown folks 5-6 and 6-7 in high tech companies. failure points with people + what did you learn. empathy is key for people leaders at fb. 

5/leading@ : done by m1/m2/D TPMs. comms + story telling, diverse + inclusive practices, stakeholder management, situational leadership (coach/direct), self-learning abilities, proactiveness & drive,

messages.content:
Hey All, I have an upcoming interview for stripe TPM. Wanted to check how is the billing team vs payments team vs support connect
Also how is the culture since I have heard mixed reviews and any insight on the interview process and tips.
Hi Fam! Any advice or resources I could refer to for FB's hiring manager interview for TPM role?

They typically try to cover a lot of ground. The yardstick is if there's a >50% chance that you'll make it thru the onsite loops. There's 3 axes that they look for signal on : leadership, program management and technical. For more senior levels, leadership is the most important axis. 
For more junior levels , your program management and tech skills need to shine. 
There'll most likely also be a 10 min mini system design type question to see if you're approaching that in the right way.

Prepare PgM 101 stuff - dependency, risk, comms, stakeholder management, etc. 
Tech - be prepared to talk about the tech details of a project you ran.. the more complex, the better. 
Sys design - practice doing an abridged 10min version
Don't keep talking for 5 min.. keep answers short and sweet, but also look for feedback if the interviewer wants u to go into more details.
If you've worked in bottoms up organizations, def highlight that experience
Thank you!!

Had a question today during an interview... if you're managing a team of TPMs and you have a TPM that is strong in software but managing a project to build a new data center; how do you support them since the team is giving feedback in the lack of domain expertise and communication.

Was a curve ball, but went first to empathy, to seek to understand from the TPM and the team members giving feedback, ensuring we had the right root cause of the feedback.  Then to focus on coaching the TPM to create a plan, in this case focus on getting them the knowledge/learning about the domain on data center design and build out, while co-creating a communication strategy on the project.
would also be good to understand why it happened in the first place. did you as the leader put them there, or was it something you inherited. do you have options to switch people out?
Yeah I was wondering that but wasn't sure that was a good move in the interview to ask that
I would ask - giving the interviewer a peek into your thought process that you'd consider all aspects before making a decision?

1) Solve the today's problem and next steps  
2) Root cause the program / TPM matching process to help avoid repeating this
Focus - >pivot - > PIP! Do it the Amazon way 😀 😀
Yeah no kidding lol… this company culture is probably the complete opposite of Amazon from what I can tell
You comfortable sharing which co?
Hubspot
@HK @Green Music @TheTofuTerror any of you have a different take here?

Didn’t really have much to add.. All given parameters in that scenario seem to fit well together but the outcome is bad. I think I always like suggestions in no win scenarios that are like Kirk’s Kobayashi Maru solutions where you ask if you have inherited the solution or something where this is maybe an issue of perspective or maybe an issue related to other parameters (conflict, basic mismatches, unrealistic expectations for time etc) since complaints are rarely one sided… but yeah I’d observe the situation (probably already know about it since I’m so invested) and decide based on the stage of the project on what to do. Focus - pivot - pip (with more steps since you are empathetic and helpful) sounds good to me.

I would have answered the same way as @paytun 
1. Solve the problem first - understand why/ what areas the TPM is not doing well.
My teams are encouraged to pick up programs that offer some learning exp and challenges. So I would need to spend sometime to understand what areas are causing friction. 
2. Then I would offer help/ support depending on the findings - get another junior person to help with the status updates and communication, or a domain expert to help with the risk/dependency assessment and management . 
And would personally keep monitoring the program more closely to ensure the plan is working .

I made reference to this book, The Coaching Habit from Michael Bungay Stanier (great book btw, link:https://boxofcrayons.com/the-coaching-habit-book/) on just not telling the TPM what to do, rather helping them come up with the plan or co-creating it with them.
when i first went from IC to manager, my default in 1:1s was "if i were you, here's what i'd do..."   and that wasn't the right thing and realized it w/ that book
Thanks - I’ve been looking for good coaching guidance
@mac @AG  would love to get your thoughts or feedback on my answer above, in an interview setting .
I always say ‘if this was something I was dealing with’ 😂 because it’s different and I’m not ‘you’ but you know the only thing of value I have are my experiences
Tomatopotato?

In the book it'll talk about the importance of that person coming up with the plan/idea, since there's a 70% they likely know... and when they do, they are learning / more passionate / committed to it... ultimately if they don't get it, you try to guide them there, and if that still won't work you can do that as the 3rd option

Everyone pretty much covered the best approach. Diagnose the root of the gap, co create an improvement plan, set clear milestones for success, and monitor for results. 

The wildcard for me would be to assess the team delivering the feedback about the TPM. If there are any politics or frictions or historical dynamics running beneath the surface that are impeding your TPM, those need to be sniffed out too. Classic example is an Eng partner who doesn’t believe in the role, wants it to fail. Not common, but I’ve seen it happen.
Interesting. How did you uncover the saboteur in the past and how did you course correct?

It wasn’t exactly subtle, but would crop in things like withholding information, making decisions to better their teams deliverables at the expense of the program goals, etc. feedback from their peers to me also exposed their intentions. At the end of the day, I had to do a sit down to walk through their perspective. Turns out they had misunderstood the role of TPM to drive decisions, not always make them, and were worried about losing control of their team and goals. They were also just a difficult and contrarian personality to begin with. With better alignment, the partnership improved over time.
I would definitely add what @TheTofuTerror  said to the answer. Diagnose if there is a problem to be solved in the first place, and what/where the exact problem is. 🙂

Had an interview in the the semi past that asked what steps I'd take to retire an old api where you didn't know everyone who was using it... here's some notes on it if you encounter a similar question:
Announce the end-date on your website's blog, and email developers (if you have their contact info) to let them know about the upcoming end date.
In your API docs, include a message/banner on all pages which mentions the end-date. This will help alert developers who may arrive there via a deep link from a search.

Hey all, jfyi i'll be conducting a mock interview (behavioral) with @HK . Will be recording and providing for the group.

Follow up - we completed the mock, but agreed not to record given we didn't want to disclose @HK's identity via the specific examples. 

Key Feedbacks
. Better ice-breakers into the discussion
. During the "tell me about yourself" go beyond the resume 
. During "tell me about a time when" questions use the STARR method (situation, action, task , result, reflection)
. on Conflict, failures - be ready to admit you failed but also how you learned and grew...
. Make sure to tell stories - keep the information light and to the point. no rambling
. practice all your stories, write them down and memorize them. Break them down into key areas... like Amazon's LPs. have 2-3 stories each
. Failure stories are much more powerful (IMHO) than success stories... they give you an opportunity to show when you really had to rollup the sleeves, resolve conflict, deal with failure and learn + grow
. keep your poise / body language. smile! make it fun!
@HK and @Ultra Instinct I respect that decision
But would ask you to share the questions asked and anonymized answers (if possible)

Mock Behavioral Interview

1. Tell me about yourself
2. why facebook?
3. what is your proudest professional moment at work?
4. Tell me about a time you managed a technical project?
5. Could you explain to me how you manage a project risk + examples?
6. Tell me about a time you had a conflict and you were wrong / had your mind changed
7. Tell me about a time you had a project failure and how you managed?
8. Tell me your approach to kicking off a new project
9. When you retire, and all your co-workers are present, what will they all miss about you? what will they not miss?

so in this case, 
1/ what else do we know about the feedback?
2/ is this coming from across the spectrum or certain segments/categories only? 
3/ is this only in certain geos. 
4/ is this only happening with SMBs led/owned by certain demographics? (ie, is there bias in our pricing algos/systems?)
4/ is this happening since the launch of the product or did we do something recently to cause this. 
5/ are external factors at play (covid impacting SMBs disproportionately) 
6/ do people understand how to use the product correctly? 
...........

hey! not sure if these were answered.. anyways, here are my 2 cents : 
1/ Play it according to the time you have. Also calibrate based on what the interviewer has already told you (keep answers short, etc.). Ideally if you can give a 10 sec intro to the overall product, great.. like so "this example is from when I had to rollout X feature in Y country; 5 engineering teams were involved". and then go into STAR. Remember to clearly disambiguate between what "YOU" did vs what the team collectively did; do not overuse the "we" word. 
2/ it is good to see that you have experience in traditional project management tools and methodologies. Depending upon the nature of the organization you are interviewing with, that could be an advantage. but also make sure to call out clearly that you will need to tailor the approaches to that org's culture. If you go in too rigid with your approaches, the first 6 mos will be torrid and you might never enjoy work.
Thank you.! Much Appreciated.

I recently interviewed people for a Senior TPM role. Some of the metrics / questions that I was checking on were below. Some are pretty obvious : 
1. Communication skills :  candidate was able to provide a a concise and structured answer. (Some candidates tend to get verbose and incoherent and this is a huge red flag to me) 

2. Scope and Impact : Candidate has experience leading programs of large scope and complexity (measured by # of teams / stakeholders managed, business value add, length of program) 

4. Driver : can the candidate cut through ambiguity and effectively deliver programs? Are they proactive about risks? 

4. Building Trust - Candidate demonstrates the ability to gain trust and credibility with a team. (IMO this is a very important trait for a PgM in order to create and drive successful programs)

5. Negotiator : ability to get resources needed, unblock items and motivate teams to cross the finish line 

6. Empathetic : can this person work with different people, bring teams together, listen and address concerns?
Not sure if this is the level of detail you were looking for

Not sure if this is the best place to ask but I wanted to ask for help on answering 2 interview questions. I’m trying to find a good framework. The questions are how would you go about navigating if a project timeline is being asked to be accelerated (perhaps by stakeholders/clients), and also if it’s delayed (unforeseen circumstances, or failure of team planning). I’m trying to find good frameworks to answer these questions. If you feel comfortable DMing me with any insight I’d really appreciate it!
I would approach both these questions on the lines of : 1. Impact assessment , 2. prioritization along  with stakeholders 3, stakeholder management

1. Impact assessment - what is impact of accelerated or delayed timeline. Why is that? In case of acceleration, is there a market driven need that is driving it? Knowing why will help with the next step

2. Prioritization - If there is a business need driving the change then its of utmost importance to understand that. With that - you will work with your team to understand what will it take for the team to deliver priorities and the tradeoffs. You will then need to use these inputs to work with the stakeholders to be clear and on the same page about the priorities and tradeoffs /

3. Stakeholder mgmt -  to ensure the expectations are set right esp w.r.t. the tradeoffs - things that were expected to get done but will not be done anymore since we traded them off to have something else of more value
@Sito  see above 👆 ... hope this helps
Thank you!! This was very helpful and provides a very clear and easy to digest framework. I appreciate it 😄
In terms of impact assessment, I would assess cost, schedule, resource and quality impacts against acceleration or delay.

Also @Sito , to add to what @hercules and @BanjoBowl  said I would also clarify what "accelerating" means? We can deliver the bare minimum (mvp) in the first release and  iterate via continuous planning - the charm of being agile. We can accelerate the internal release (sprints) and still keep the GA same. Say your project starts in January. Beta--> June and GA-->November. You can still keep these dates and accelerate the feature delivery (after prioritizing with PM)by releasing MVP in Beta and the 2nd set of priority in GA. Other features post GA.... To give an example, I had to deliver a feature which was planned in GA in beta phase. We delivered the UI in beta and the full functionality during GA. This expedited user adoption in GA....

Understand the other parties perspective and story. Present your case and try to find common ground. Consider pros and cons of both approaches. If you cannot come to a meeting of the minds with give and take, escalate to leaderships of both parties to discuss

I always emphasize the need to be a story-teller as a TPM. Being able to tell a clear story of the program big picture and how each individual fits into. Make clear the vision and people are more likely to get on board. Show them how they’re a valuable resource for the program to be successful
Set clear expectation once they are on board and continue to drive alignment over the course of it.

If you can link any experiences or stories to one of these principles explained in the video. One principle is Reciprocity - which means help first before asking for any specific deliverable. When someone receives something not expected they usually respond to any further requests in a positive manner. Building a foundation of trust with the team member is essential.

For anyone who's thinking about moving to EPM/TPM. Here's what I posted in the #hardware-tpms channel:

1) Work alongside TPMs and make sure you really like the R&R (roles & responsibilities).
2) Find internal opportunities for managing a program. Official title change is nice (easier in smaller companies) but it's the experience that matters. 
3) Keep notes about how your day to day R&R help move a program forward as it'd be critical for your resume/interview later.
4) Start applying for TPM roles once you deliver one or two programs and feel ready to convince others why you'd make a good TPM.
5) Focus on the job that has the best match to your technical background as you can't compete on PM experience.
6) Expect a ton of rejections and no-replies since recruiters are swamped with resume from people with TPM titles.
7) Rehearse your interviews and do mocks as much as possible.
8) Repeat 4) to 7) until you get the offer you want.

TPMs...I need advice on how to answer the question "How would you manage a Program end to end". I want to ask what everyone else's experience has been with getting an ambiguous goal and running with it? What I am trying to ask is  do I answer this question that involves several steps that happen before an official project kickoff like getting a product manager involved, getting a team involved, writing the objectives, charter, making sure your funding and resource request is approved before actually engaging the engineers (at-least that is how we do it in my non FAANG organization). Or should I make these "pre-planning" part the initiation and then talk about further planning, execution and monitoring, closure? No right answer I guess, I know it varies even within orgs in a larger corportation.
How to ideally tackle the question "Tell me about a time when you were new on the job and had a lot to learn"?

are you in a product organization ? I believe what you are describing might not be how most product/tech companies might kick off a product effort. 
it'll usually start with company objectives >> goals >> KPIs >> each product getting some direction on what they should focus on >> a combo of bottoms up and tops down planning on the most important initiatives, high level funding/staffing required >> submitting a plan back to leadership, getting headcount approved (which is usually < what was asked) and then deciding which are the most impactful things to fund. in most prod&tech companies, this is all done by Product and Eng leadership. Usually every initiative will then be assigned a PM. and then when you start as a TPgM, you usually take the product strategy and goals and build out an execution plan. 

I'd say that you should caveat the answer you described as the way it is currently being done in the org -- and that you understand that it will need to be adjusted to the culture of the org -- bottoms up vs tops down, etc.

1/ what are the different mechanisms/forums you tapped into for learning
2/ how did you prioritize what you learned and how you spent your time
3/ how did you pay it forward

messages.content: Can someone point to how we define success metrics for a project as TPM

This is how I would’ve approached and feedback is welcomed. 1/ understand the purpose/goal of the program, why are we doing this? 2/ identify what would success look like? 3/ identify the types of metrics - business/product/system 4/ define specific metrics and identify why are they important eg Business metric - ROI why is ROI needed, 4/ decide how /who/when to measure 5/ monitoring and controlling program
Makes sense, thank you.

I’m in HW, and the metrics (some are implicit) I’ve seen in the past are:

1) are you able to deliver your part on time? This is more important than anything else

2) are you able to get cross-functional alignment on what to deliver? A product with 70% x-functional support is better than a spec with 100% support

3) how much money do you spend to deliver your part compared to the budget? This is where “pricing power” comes in. A TPM who has more power to exaggerate the budget has the advantage.

4) how many bridges do you build/burn to deliver your part? The importance of this depends on the performance review style.

Here's sharing some high level overview of what is assessed for leadership TPM roles
**People Management **- org design, career development, performance management, hiring and retaining top talent
**Partnership & Communication** - Stakeholder management, effective communication, relationship building, influence, Conflict management
**Behavioral **- Culture, Values, career path, growth mindset, community contributions
**Program Management** - Design strategies and executing complex programs, leading large programs working with many stakeholders, leading programs with/through others. 

Besides the people management one, the others are similar in subject to IC roles - however, the examples/stories you provide need to be related to your time as a manager/leader and how your addressed situations.

Few folks reached to me last week to learn about CB interview prep as they’re preparing for the same so thought to post a summary here. Hopefully it'll help aspiring TPMs who have just started preparing for TPM interview. I'll not share typical interview questions as we've plenty in our discord. This is what my prep looked like besides already available contents in TPM Unite Guide.

Program Mngmnt: I spent least time on this as i consider this as my strongest side. I had my PMP 11 yrs back but Google Project Management Certification was a great refresher. I'll highly recommend this to brush up Project Management basics - it took me <1 week to complete the course in a fast forward manner. It's available in coursera.

Leadership & Partnership: I consciously trained myself to follow servant leadership for years at my work. I'm a big fan of Simon Sinek and his podcasts - His perspective on leadership & teamwork is amazing. Since April, i also started listening to TPM Podcast by Mario. He hosts PM, TPM & leadership folks from FAANG and these were super helpful to educate myself on ways of working at FAANG and helped to answer better during the interviews. I usually listened to them when I’m driving/go out for walk.

System Design: This is the area, i worked most. I consider myself technical enough to lead program effectively but i didn't have practical experieince to work on FAANG type projects (chat system, social network, payment system for millions...). I didn’t have ideas about system design, when I started my interview process. I started with different youtube videos to learn different solutions and when i went for Meta Connectivity TPM interview in January, i failed miserably in System design part. My approach to solve the problem was wrong. I was trying to learn different solutions watching different youtube videos and i had no idea what to do when the problem is unknown. I knew i need to work on this area and started with Alex Xu's part 1 for a month to learn the basics and how to address a problem. Alex Xu's part two is too heavy and it won't help much if you have limited time. What helped me most is watching  @Ultra Instinct videos here in discord. He has 03 videos (Whatsapp, food delivery & Hotel reservations) in discord. I watched them again and again (5+ times each) and followed his way of solving the problem. This is when i realized that if i know basic components and know how to put them together explaining the reasoning, i would be able to solve most of the problems. I was so much influenced by his videos that i literally followed his drawing style in each of my interview and  could secure offer from MS, AWS & CB.

Again, I'm grateful to this awesome TPM community and More specifically thankful to @AG , @a_tpm & @Ultra Instinct  - you guys are the BEST. 🙏🏽

@bambam911  copied from another channel      in my limited but recent experience... this is how I'm perceiving things out of 7 interviews (1 recruiter, 1 phone screen and 5 on-site):
- 3 no-go's - you're likely done, no offer.
- 2 no-go's - could be over with no offer OR could be sent to the hiring committee where you may be extended an offer, but very likely down-leveled.  HC could even decide to re-interview one or more portions before deciding.
- 1 no-go - won't be over, but you will be sent to the hiring committee who then decides what level your offer would be including down-leveled, at level or up-leveled (although unlikely).  Could also be re-interviewed.
- 0 no-go - sent to hiring committee and will likely get an offer at level or even up-leveled (likely)

Hi, I am a tech lead who does technical program management as well 60% of the time as my current role. I am running 2 programs with 30 engineers. I would like to grow as a TPM. I did the entire loop at  Amazon for a Sr. TPM role. But I failed to convince them that I can do the job. Any suggestions?
Should I apply for a TL role or a TPM role?

Seems like you're taking it to heart. Don't. You just need to crack that 1 interview..not all of them. Also note that an interview is not a judgement of what you have done in all your years. So don't think you're not good enough. It is a separate preparation strategy. So that means your strategy didn't work.

TPM interviews have a behavioral part and a technical part. As mentioned above, find your weak area.. for behavioral, you need to build stories from your exp that project certain qualities that Amazon looks for, and then communicate those stories effectively(star format). For tech, you need to study system design concepts and practice some real world systems, until you are fluent in the content as well as delivery.
If factually you have the experience and scope size for the programs then perhaps try to understand what areas you may be perceived as lacking?
Behavioural or communication etc

Does anyone have a good framework for articulating program complexity? I took a first pass at some key areas, but it would be great to know how others select their 'most complex programs' to discuss in interviews.

**PROGRAM SCOPE / COMPLEXITY**

**Business/Strategic Influence:
**TPM contributions to program/product roadmap
Discovery for use cases or business objectives
Define problem/design solution in ambiguity

**Organizational: **
Cross-functional: influencing without direct authority, driving initiatives across internal and/or external teams and stakeholders

# of projects : [ is there a better way to characterize this?]

Team: mix of roles  (technical experts, domain experts, etc ) and number of members

**Technical/Architectural: **
Domain knowledge
Scale
Performance requirements
Technical constraints


**Product Metrics/Impact: **
# of users
revenue

the dimensions we use to think thru program complexity (and hence staff the right level of TPM to it) : 
* Impact (business metrics, strategic bets, etc.) 
* Scope  (# of teams, # (and levels) of people, internal/external stakeholders, etc.)
* Complexity (x-org dependencies, constraints, etc.) 
* Ambiguity (0-1 problems, new space, clarity of problems and solutions) 
* Technical/Domain knowledge (how deeply tech or domain specific the solution is going to be)

Hi ya'll, I wanted to get inout from others about "How do you measure a program's success?"

I have my ideas, but wanted to understand how our fellow TPMs are measuring their program's success.

Every question in TPM interview is about the STAR story. Even though the question is “how do you do…..?”, it’s actually asking “how did you do it in the past?”

That said, I’d give an example of the program I managed in the past and how I set up metrics with stakeholders so we could quickly visualize the progress and challenges along the way.

Adding to what @Borderline88 said, get in the specifics about what you decided to measure, how you decided to measure (automated as manual cant be sustainable), at what intervals and how these measurements were used to pivot/course correct. Success criteria/measurements can be at various levels- north star metrics (top 3 metrics - most likely this will be business centric like %growh in accounts opened/revenue, or turnaround time in loan processing etc), secondary metrics can be tech metrics like platform availability/uptime, resource utilization, user experience metrics etc, and last but not the least program mgmt metrics- how did you manage triple constraints (cost, Effort, scope).
Thanks so much yall!

@Waytogo, 
My Interview Experience - **Walmart Principal TPM in Information Security**
100% Onsite, Atlanta (GA). Interviewed across Feb & Mar.

**Format** - 1 Recruiter round + 1 Hiring Manager discussion + 2 Full Loop sessions

**Differentiators** wrt other company interviews
1) Full loops comprised of 5 interviewers (Sr Dirs & above) in each panel
2) Full loops were intense rapid fire + follow up questions => Answering “100% in STARL” is not always possible.
3) 2 to 3 weeks between each interview outcome announcement.

**Sequence**
1)** 30 mins Recruiter Round** - Usual role fitment + non technical questions.
**Suggestion** - Highlight JD relevant work experience as a standard.
2)** 1 Hour Hiring Manager Conversation** - Deep dive into current and prior program’s e2e execution + high level technical questions on security, risk and compliance.
3) **1 Hour Non-Tech Panel** - In the allocated 50 mins, each of the 5 interviewers will ask at-least 4 questions fit enough to answer in detail in STARL but time is a constraint. Covered e2e program management, stakeholder conflicts, risk impacts, failures in career / failed program etc
**Suggestion **- Practice traditional non STARL format as well, to invoke if necessary.
4) **1 Hour Tech Panel** - In the allocated 50 mins, solve the 1 line problem statement to design a system. Each of the 5 interviewers asked multiple questions covering NFRs and security in specific, data breach, containerization, backup and recovery mechanism, identity management etc.
**Suggestion** - Study NFRs from JD in depth.

Hey everyone, I’ve applied for a PM position at Amazon and they sent me an Online Assessment Test, Could anybody tell me what all can I expect in that test? 

Thanks much 😊

This is typically the expectations for entry level TPMs >> 
**Scope:** You operate primarily within the scope of your program with high level guidance from your manager, or a more senior TPM.
**Complexity:** Can explain as well as communicate information; asks questions to understand others' PoVs
**Discretion:** Uses professional concepts and company processes/practices to execute projects
**Interaction:** works on problems of limited scope, but importance. Interacts mostly with team and project level members
**Supervision:** Normally receives detailed instructions about work, but customarily exercises discretion and independent judgement on matters of importance
you should be able to convince the interviewers that you can do these things well and that you have solid examples demonstrating these traits and landed impact.

Hi all, currently preparing for a final round TPM interview at Stripe. Does any one have resources they could share regarding system design? Would be greatly appreciated 🙏🏾

from a Stripe TPM : Yes there are 2 technical rounds at Stripe. System thinking which is System design interview with a TPM and focussed on a generic system. It's hour long, so expectation is to gather requirments, define goals and metrics, define the high level system, discuss trade-offs and go in-depth in some areas based on how the discussion progresses. Since it's with a TPM, it's not "as technical" as it was at say Amazon with a staff engineer

Thanks AG! Yeah this one of the segments of the final round but I wasn't sure the depth to prepare for or potential call outs. I actually haven't done a system design interview before even at Amazon so just wanted to make sure I'm not too underprepared. Below is the list of what they gave me. 

1. Technical Discussion [45 min]: This interview with an EM or EMoM is a discussion based on your written project. During this interview, you should focus on the technical aspects of your program rather than the program management aspects.


2. Systems Thinking [60 min]: This interview with a TPM is a classic systems design interview with a whiteboard. You will be given a prompt, and you should ask questions, discuss trade-offs and the why's. 

3. Program Management [45 min]: This behavioral interview with the hiring manager evaluates your planning process, your framework for execution tracking, how you communicate to every level of a program/stakeholder, and how you manage the end to end process on programs with high technical proficiency. 

4. Working with Engineering and Product [45 min]: This interview with a SWE or Product Manager evaluates how you lead with clarity and context and how you both teach and learn from others.

5. Ownership and Drive [45 min]: This behavioral interview with a PM evaluates your growth mindset, how you solve ambiguous and vague problems, how you can lead and improve culture on your team, how you seek feedback and your ability to solve problems.

messages.content: If you are following Agile scrum methodology, ideally you are supposed to have backlog for each project for 3 months advance available in JIRA and by setting up regular cadence meeting to prioritize the backlog items with your Product Manager/Dev teams/QA Teams/Infra teams/stake holders  you get alignment on the priority of the backlog items with everyone involved.  If you follow all Agile ceremonies (Daily scrum/Planning/Demos/Retro) most of the team members will understand the priority of the tasks they need to work on each sprint and an alignment on priority items on backlog items is done looking at the Business value/urgency/Impact by PO/Dev architects/QA Leads. The senior leadership executives decide the priority on org level priority items (EPIC level).

I’ve been leveraging WSJF to great effect wherever people will let me try it. I’ve found it to be incredibly well received in MOST cases once people have seen it in action. The goal is to keep it light. No more than a few hours per quarter.

Some questions that folks on my ex-team got while interviewing at a few different places. 

- Tell me about a complex program you ran - gets asked every time
- ⁠Tell me about a time you had to give difficult feedback 
- ⁠Tell me about a time you received difficult feedback 
- ⁠ Tell me about an ambiguous problem you solved
- ⁠Tell me about a time you had to make (technical) tradeoffs
- ⁠Tell me about a program that was not going well / not on track
- who were your stakeholders for (a given program) - I focus on defining which groups of stakeholders I have and how I tailor comms and level of details for each 
- ⁠how do you get disparate team aligned on your programs mission/vision/timeline
* tell me about a time you worked with a difficult stakeholder
- What part of our company’s values resonates with you? 

Some other things that have been helpful for me
- storytelling and checking in - esp when describing a technical program from start to finish, I would share my story in chunks and pause to let the interviewer probe or ask questions. Also prevents me from rambling
- I used HelloInterview to study system design (the AI mock interviews are super helpful) and their Story Builder tool to build my STAR stories. The tool asks for a lot of followup questions which also helped me jog my memory of my past work.

* Tell me about a time when you proposed a solution that was turned down by leadership
* Tell me about a time when you disagreed to something and you were wrong
* What is the most technical solution you proposed?
* How effective are OKRs in helping teams achieve their goals? In what situations would they fail?
* What would you change in your working style to be successful at xyz company?
* What are your most successful tactics in influencing teams and achieving alignment? Follow up: What do you think the ingredients are for successful alignment between dev teams and product teams?
* Tell me about a time when you had to manage multiple projects with competing priorities? How did you prioritize and what was the impact of your decision?
* Tell me about a time when you identified and mitigated a significant risk in your project
* Tell me about a program you led that exceeded expectations
* How do you keep up with the latest trends and technologies in your field?

Several people recently asked about Meta Partnerships interviews. I conduct these interviews pretty regularly so I'll share some info here. 
TPM Partnerships interview is to assess how well you are able to establish __and leverage__ relationships with cross functional partners as part of your work as well as effectively communicate with different types and levels of audiences.     
I usually divide my interviews into three sections: 

1. **Collaboration with XFN** - how you build and foster relationships with stakeholders in your programs (technical, non technical, leadership, etc.) and how you deal with challenging people. In this part I am looking for your ability to connect with program stakeholders, understand what drives them and how you partner with them to get them aligned to the program and business goals. Empathy is very important here, I want to see that you consider how *other people feel* and what makes them tick so you can get them to collaborate with you. What I don't want to see is "I wrote a document and asked their leadership to commit resources to the program". It's not that it's wrong, some companies work that way but Meta is a bottom up culture and I need to see how you work with people directly.

2. **Effective communication** - how you communicate your ideas to people and to different type of audiences. Here I'm looking for the media you use for formal communications like emails, chats, written newsletters, slides, etc. What meetings you hold, how frequently and with whom. How you leverage informal communications (hallway conversations, coffee break, slack, etc.) to hear what people really think and make sure extended stakeholders and leadership are kept up to date on your program. 


3. **Influence without authority** - this one is more squishy. Here I want to see how you leverage relationships to get people to want to work on your ideas and programs. This can range from setting multi-stakeholder meetings to align on priorities of an upcoming program to 1:1s with leaders to get them to support your ideas before going into a decision meeting. How you use data and business impact to convince people what you're working on is important. For seniors: how you lean on your relationships with leadership to line up support for your ideas and priorities. Using escalations is ok, in moderation when all other options have been exhausted or maybe when you've worked with a person and agreed they need some help on resources or priorities from their manager. What I don't want to see is: "I file a task in JIRA for person A to do something" or "I heard this person wasn't cooperative so I went to their manager directly"

This is good data.  Could you please provide the best answer structure / format to formulate responses for the System Design questions?  Surely these cannot be as detailed as the SDM type of loop…

I think the trick here is to understand how long you have to answer the question, and what aspects of the overall system the interviewer is most interested in hearing from you. A 10min response to a system design question in a screening round is going to look very different from a dedicated System design 45min interview. 

In a screening round, I usually look for signals if the candidate understand different parts of the system they are talking about, e.g. API Gateway, LBs, SQL/noSQL db choices, micro services, and challenges around scaling, DR etc. I'd recommend you look at this System design cheatsheet and familiarize yourself against all these parts. Does anyone have any resources or script recommendations for how to answer the “tell me about yourself” question? As someone who switched industries and career path, I’ve still not settled on a way to tackle this question authentically.

Talk about your skills, strengths and what you are passionate about in the role, make sure you are weaving in the keywords from the Job description. Touch on the impact you have created lightly.  You don’t want to give away every title or company but enough for the interviewer to be intrigued. Write it down, voice record your intro and asses as a 3rd person for further improvements.
This is great advice - thank you so much!!

I will share a slightly different approach that works for me. When I get asked this question, I do my best to introduce *new* information, rather than just walking them through my resume. I'd say something like "If you like, I can walk through the lines on the resume, but I'll assume you've already read that. 15 years in the industry across a range of companies and technologies, most recently at _"

Then like TPM above, I'd redirect to strengths and passion depending on what I feel I still need to highlight. For example: "What you might not see in the resume is why I choose to be a TPM instead of an SDE, SDM, or PM. I love being close to the technology, and the TPM role allows me to do that while leveraging my strengths in communication and organization. One specific example of doing that  is ______"

As a personal nitpick, I think it's a green flag when I hear a candidate state they opt into being a TPM, rather than as a stepping stone to PdM or SDM. (Of course, only say this if it's congruent with your actual beliefs. Faking interest will backfire)

Or I might choose to discuss a connection to the business domain. "What my resume doesn't show is that I love teaching and education. I tutored a lot in high school and college, and spend a lot of time doing mentorship now. If I weren't in tech, I'd probably be a professor somewhere. That's why I'm interested in this EdTech role ...."

That said, it's always good to give the interviewer the option if what they want you to focus on is the resume summary, hence my opening statement.

I never had any experience with the failed program. How do you answer this question? What can be the reasons? Is it necessary that program is failed due to wrong project management or planning or wrong decision? Can we consider something which was changed due to business decision?

I think the assumption is that if you are senior enough then you must have been involved in a program that failed in some way. Most program miss some of the objectives like pushed out the deadline, increased budget or change in scope.  You can probably talk to them about how do they define a failure and if they say it is up to you can propose something.
Ok so missing deadline, budget or scope can be called as failure and not necessarily complete project failure.
That is how I answer, but maybe other have different opinions. Also make sure to prepare for the follow up question " What would you do differently ?"

Hmm, it’s interesting, because I would expect a senior TPM to have some experience in a decent failure, I.e program or feature is cancelled partway thru because not making progress(technical or schedule), or it launches and doesn’t meet business goals etc.

If this truly has never happened to you, then definitely just try and think about the closest thing to a failure you’ve had, as mentioned above. I wouldn’t pick an example of  something done TO you (I.e. VP decided to change strategy and cancel a program so you have to react), that never gave me signal when I interviewed people. As long as it wasn’t a gross error in judgement by the TPM, failures are OK.

I think a huge part of being a TPM is being aware of when things are going wrong and having those lived-in experiences is crucial.

Yes definitely. I can find out those examples based on my experience. Though it’s not complete project failure, we definitely have something which was failed while driving the initiative.
Doesn’t meet business goal after launch will be a perfect example according to me. But difficult to answer follow up question- what will you do differently?

I don’t think anyone can give you that answer. You need to find the big takeaway from your specific example. In general if a product didn’t meet goals then think back to when those goals were established and what analysis was done that made folks think it would succeed

Yes, this seems like a good example. For the follow up you should think about retrospective post launch, why it didn't meet the goal, was there a scope creep or planning that went wrong. Or you could look at options about trade offs that didn't work the program or was it really an aggressive goal set without backing it by data or measurement mechanism

what are some of the questions you should ask to the interviewer when the interview is not for a specific role? There will be a team matching process if I clear all rounds. @AG @CaliMan @Cekis

Assuming the interviewer is not a hiring manager, you can ask generic questions about the role or the company.
- what would success look like in this role at 30/90/180 days
- what is the decision making process in the company?
- what have some of the more successful people in the role done that impressed the interviewer
- what area of the company do they work in and how is it aligned with company priorities

Try to frame your question with why you are asking it.  For example: “As this is an open position that could be with any number of groups, how would you look at someone in this position and say they have been successful?” Then continue that line: “what criteria would you use to measure their success?”
It’s best to recognize the situation (unspecific) and gain that insight on what success looks like from those that are measuring your ability to actually be successful.

For the TPM/EPM interviews, if there is a general timing guidance for each interview questions, could you please share?

I seem to ramble sometimes during the interview even after following the STAR method.
I would like to time myself according to the timing guidance if there is any.

I'd aim to keep it for 3 min or less. I practice using the stop watch to make sure I don't cross that. Initially when I used to practice using the stop watch, I used to feel rushed, but later  I improved. A general rule of thumb (verbatim, courtesy: AWS recruiter): 

---
The 10/80/10 Rule:

There is no perfect formula on how long a response should be. This will depend on the complexity of the example; however the time you spend sharing an example should be as follows:

About 10% of your response should focus on the Situation and Task (beginning of your story)
About 80% of your response should focus on your Actions (middle of the story)
About 10% of your response should focus on the Results (end of the story)
---

make sure show learnings/takeaways from the story, I call it STAR-T, where the last T is takeaways. 

Also, I've seen in most of my interviews, I got interrupted when telling my story, in a good way.  This was very much across all of my Apple EPM and AWS interviews. I felt the interviewer were leading me to the skills they wanted me to evaluate, and questioned me - why/how/what/etc.  

my $0.02, if your story is going to be too long based on the Q, set expectations w/ your interviewer that you plan to present A, B and C aspects part of the story. For example, ***Tell me a most complex Program you led E2E***. Now this can go for several minutes to even an hour. You'd need to set expectation with your interviewer saying, you plan to go through the high level phase say Initiation, Planning ....... Closure ,with emphasis on the Execution where you had the most contribution.  Hope this helps, good luck.

Hi, I am trying to figure out if I should get enrolled in a course like AI product management with Jyoti Nookula where they does a 5 week E2E walk through of building AI products along with 6 hands on projects. I am getting back to interviews after a maternity break and trying to figure out the best way to navigate prep for interviews. I am a Hw & SW TPM and open to both Product and TPM roles. I already have access to product alliance full course work but still feel I need mentoring as have not been able to crack hiring manager/ technical screens

Having tried a few tools and attended trainings, I'd say the best bet is to download Claude, pay for a premium membership and then go build stuff yourself .. and these are ideally tools or products that you yourself would use. You talked about coming back from maternity - are there tools /products/features you'd build as a parent for eg.
And then instead of saying you did a training, you can link to an actual webapp or product in your resume
That’s great advice. Thank you. Received very similar feedback from another TPM and it made lot of sense

Hiring is also probably going to change, so getting familiar with using tools to build stuff will become tablestakes I think .. see what Wealthsimple did recently.. 
https://newsroom.wealthsimple.com/we-asked-canadians-to-build-something-instead-of-sending-a-resume-heres-what-happened
I liked their approach as well as the retrospective they did on their process, it’s definitely better than the typical resume-interview approach
